title: Owner can steal all ERC20 tokens in the contract via acquire()
reason: `acquire()` only withdraws assets already held by the token contract itself; the contract has no deposit accounting, so this is a stray-asset recovery/admin-trust behavior rather than a vulnerability affecting tracked user balances.

title: Blacklisted users can still burn their tokens
reason: Allowing a blacklisted holder to destroy their own tokens does not let them transfer value or bypass the freeze in a way that creates realistic protocol harm.

title: Upgrade can irreversibly redirect all token operations to malicious contract
reason: Pointing upgrades at a malicious target is an owner-trust/centralization risk, not a permissionless bug; additionally, `upgrade()` is not irreversible because the owner can call it again and change `upgradedAddress`.

title: Bulk transfer functions bypass blacklist checks
reason: False positive: both bulk transfer variants eventually call `_transfer` and `_allowanceTransfer`, which enforce blacklist and pause checks.

title: Critical owner functions remain operational during pause
reason: The pause modifier is consistently applied to token-movement paths; administrative functions remaining callable is a design choice, not an exploitable protocol vulnerability by itself.

title: Immutable DOMAIN_SEPARATOR breaks permit on chain forks
reason: This is a speculative fork-compatibility concern rather than a realistic in-scope vulnerability causing present protocol harm.

title: Missing event emissions for role management
reason: This is a monitoring/transparency issue only and does not create direct protocol-level harm.

title: acquire() allows stealing ETH from contract
reason: Same root issue as the ERC20 `acquire()` candidate: it only recovers ETH held by the contract itself, with no user accounting or payable deposit flow to violate.
